Used Car Pricing in the Detroit 100-Mile Market

Author

Omar Hijazi

1 Introduction

This report analyzes used-car dealer listings within a 100-mile radius of Detroit, Michigan, collected from the MarketCheck Cars API free tier. The dataset includes four high-volume brands: Toyota, Honda, Ford, and Chevrolet. The analysis focuses on listing price, mileage, vehicle age, and key vehicle attributes such as body type, drivetrain, and transmission.

The goals of the project are to:

  1. Identify which characteristics best predict listing price,
  2. Determine whether the relationship between mileage and price differs by brand,
  3. Test whether body type, drivetrain, and transmission still matter after controlling for mileage and age, and
  4. Compare broad vehicle segments using body type as a proxy.

Because used-car listing prices are right-skewed, the analysis uses log(price) instead of raw price. This helps reduce the impact of unusually expensive listings, makes the model more stable, and lets the coefficients be interpreted roughly as percent changes in price.

2 Data & Preparation

The raw dataset contains 1777 observations and 27 variables. The main modeling variables include price, mileage, age, brand, body type, drivetrain, and transmission.

Next, the data is standardized for modeling. The preparation steps:

  • keep valid values (price > 0, miles >= 0, age >= 0),
  • convert key attributes into factors,
  • lump rare categories to avoid fragmentation,
  • set reference levels for interpretable comparisons, and
  • create log_price.
Final Modeling Sample Summary
Metric Value
Final observations 1777
Number of brands 4
Minimum price $1,950
Maximum price $109,995
Minimum miles 3
Maximum miles 365,037
Minimum age 0
Maximum age 31

3 Quick EDA

3.1 Log-transformed price distribution

Compared with raw price, the log-transformed distribution is more balanced and works better for linear modeling. This supports using log(price) in the regression analysis.

3.2 Log(Price) vs Miles (by brand)

log(price) goes down as mileage increases, with prices dropping more sharply at lower mileage and then leveling off more at higher mileage. The separate brand trend lines also look different, suggesting that the relationship between mileage and price may vary by manufacturer.

3.3 Log(Price) vs Age (by brand)

log(price) also drops a lot as vehicles get older. The decline is steeper in the first few years and then becomes more gradual for older vehicles, which matches normal depreciation patterns. This supports including age as an important predictor in all models.

4 Q1. Which features best predict price?

The baseline model is:

M1: log(price) ~ miles + age + make

M1 — Estimated Percent Effects
Effect Percent_Change
Mileage (+10,000 miles) -4.14
Age (+1 year) -6.43

Answer (Q1). In this model, mileage and age are the strongest predictors of listing price. In the baseline model, every additional 10,000 miles is associated with about 4.14% lower listing price, and each additional year of age is associated with about 6.43% lower listing price, holding brand constant.

The brand indicators also show clear price differences after controlling for mileage and age. Compared with Chevrolet, both Honda and Toyota are priced higher on average, while Ford is closer to Chevrolet in this baseline model. Overall, the model fits the data well for a simple specification, with Adjusted R² = 0.731, showing that mileage, age, and brand explain a large share of the variation in listing prices.

5 Q2. Does the mileage-price relationship differ by brand?

To test value retention patterns, the next model allows the mileage slope to vary by brand:

M2: log(price) ~ age + miles * make

M2 — Mileage Effect by Brand (controls: age)
make percent_drop_per_10k_miles
Chevrolet -4.75
Ford -5.81
Honda -3.31
Toyota -2.73

Answer (Q2). The relationship between mileage and price does differ by brand. Based on the estimated slopes, the price change for an additional 10,000 miles is:

  • Chevrolet: -4.75%
  • Ford: -5.81%
  • Honda: -3.31%
  • Toyota: -2.73%

A less negative value means the price drops more slowly for every additional 10,000 miles, which suggests better value retention. In this dataset, Toyota and Honda show slower mileage-related price declines than Ford and Chevrolet.

From a practical point of view, this suggests that buyers who care more about long-term value may see Toyota and Honda as better options at higher mileage, while Ford and Chevrolet may come with lower starting prices but lose value faster as mileage increases. Age also remains a strong negative predictor across all four brands, showing that both vehicle age and usage play an important role in lowering listing prices.

6 Q3. Do body type and drivetrain still matter after controlling for age and mileage?

The full-controls model is:

M3: log(price) ~ miles + age + make + body_type + drivetrain + transmission

Model Fit Comparison
Model N Adjusted_R2
Baseline (M1) 1777 0.731
Interaction (M2) 1777 0.743
Full Controls (M3) 1732 0.821
M3 — Coefficient Significance Table
Predictor Estimate Std. Error t-value p-value Sig.
miles 0.0000 0.0000 -20.474 0.0000 ***
age -0.0686 0.0024 -28.175 0.0000 ***
makeFord -0.0467 0.0205 -2.281 0.0227 *
makeHonda 0.2746 0.0227 12.073 0.0000 ***
makeToyota 0.3159 0.0212 14.887 0.0000 ***
body_typeCar Van 0.4314 0.1099 3.924 0.0001 ***
body_typeCargo Van 0.4125 0.1214 3.397 0.0007 ***
body_typeCoupe 0.5027 0.0834 6.030 0.0000 ***
body_typeHatchback -0.0055 0.0339 -0.162 0.8710 ns
body_typeMinivan 0.2776 0.0364 7.625 0.0000 ***
body_typePickup 0.4858 0.0344 14.131 0.0000 ***
body_typeSUV 0.1663 0.0217 7.666 0.0000 ***
body_typeOther 0.3745 0.0891 4.204 0.0000 ***
drivetrain4WD 0.1780 0.0204 8.711 0.0000 ***
drivetrainRWD 0.1989 0.0666 2.986 0.0029 **
transmissionCVT -0.0475 0.0216 -2.201 0.0279 *
transmissionManual -0.0454 0.0574 -0.791 0.4293 ns

The overall model is statistically significant (F(17, 1714) = 467.54, p < 0.0001), which shows that the predictors together explain a meaningful share of the variation in log(price) beyond random chance.

Looking at the individual predictors, mileage and age are highly significant (p < 0.001), confirming that they are the main drivers of price. The brand indicators for Honda and Toyota are also significant, showing clear price premiums over Chevrolet after controlling for the other variables. Among the vehicle features, SUV and Pickup body types and 4WD/RWD drivetrains have significant positive coefficients, which supports the idea that vehicle configuration still matters even after accounting for age and mileage. Transmission effects are weaker and less consistently significant than the other vehicle features.

Answer (Q3). Adding body type, drivetrain, and transmission improves the model’s explanatory power. Adjusted R² increases from 0.731 in M1 to 0.821 in M3. This suggests that vehicle configuration adds useful information beyond age, mileage, and brand.

Interpreting the main patterns in M3 relative to the reference categories (Sedan, FWD, Automatic, Chevrolet):

  • SUVs and Pickups show price premiums relative to Sedans.
  • 4WD and RWD show price premiums relative to FWD.
  • Transmission effects are smaller. CVT appears somewhat lower than Automatic, while Manual is not consistently different and may be influenced by sample size.

Overall, the full model supports the conclusion that vehicle features still play an important role in explaining price, even after controlling for age, mileage, and brand.

7 Q4. Segment differences (body type proxy)

As a descriptive comparison of segments, the following chart shows median prices by body type and brand for groups with sufficient sample size.

Answer (Q4). The descriptive summary shows clear differences across vehicle segments. SUVs and pickups tend to have higher median listing prices than sedans, which likely reflects differences in vehicle type and market demand. These differences are not only descriptive. In M3, body type still matters after controlling for mileage, age, and brand, suggesting that segment-level price differences remain even when comparing vehicles with similar age and mileage.

8 Diagnostics

The Residuals vs Fitted plot shows that the residuals are centered around zero without a strong curved pattern, which suggests the linear model is reasonable for log(price). There is some mild fanning at higher fitted values, pointing to slight heteroskedasticity, which is common in listing price data and does not make the model invalid.

The Normal Q-Q plot stays fairly close to the line in the middle, but there are some noticeable differences in the tails and a few outliers. This suggests the residuals are not perfectly normal, though the overall pattern is still acceptable for this kind of applied pricing analysis.

M3 — Variance Inflation Factors
Predictor VIF Flag
miles 2.90 OK
age 3.07 OK
make 1.20 OK
body_type 1.29 OK
drivetrain 2.28 OK
transmission 1.40 OK

VIF values below 5 suggest there is no serious multicollinearity among the predictors. Age and mileage are naturally somewhat related, but they still stay within an acceptable range. Since no predictor has a VIF above 10, multicollinearity does not appear to be a major issue in M3.

Overall, the diagnostics suggest that M3 is a reasonable model to use for interpretation in this context.

9 Data & API Notes

The listings were collected using the free tier of the MarketCheck Cars API. Since the free plan limits how many rows can be returned per query, the dataset was built by pulling each brand separately and then removing duplicates based on listing ID. The data reflects dealer asking prices at one point in time, not final sale prices.

10 Limitations

  • Listings are not transactions: asking prices may be different from the final sale prices.
  • Snapshot bias: the results only reflect the inventory that was available during the data collection period.
  • Incomplete vehicle details: trim and option information is not always fully available, so body type and drivetrain are used as broader proxies.
  • Regional scope: the results are specific to the Detroit 100-mile market and the four brands included in the analysis.

11 Key Findings

  • Mileage and age are the strongest predictors of used-car listing prices in the Detroit market.
  • Brand differences matter: in this dataset, Toyota and Honda show slower mileage-related price declines than Ford and Chevrolet.
  • Vehicle configuration still matters: SUVs, pickups, and 4WD and RWD drivetrains tend to have price premiums even after controlling for age, mileage, and brand.
  • Segment differences are consistent: SUVs and pickups are generally priced higher than sedans, both in the descriptive summary and in the regression results.

12 Conclusion

This analysis shows that used car prices in the Detroit 100-mile market are mainly affected by mileage, age, brand, and vehicle features. In this sample, Toyota and Honda seem to hold their value better as mileage goes up compared to Ford and Chevrolet. SUVs, pickups, and some drivetrain types also tend to have higher listing prices. Because prices were right-skewed, using log(price) made the regression model more stable and easier to interpret. Overall, the results show that both depreciation factors and vehicle characteristics help explain dealer listing prices in this market.
